definition: The ABAP statement embedded in code that evaluates a user's authorization buffer against a specific authorization object and field values, setting sy-subrc accordingly.
A template of up to 10 fields grouping related authorization checks, e.g. M_MSEG_WMB for goods movements.
A single field slot within an authorization object, such as ACTVT (Activity) or WERKS (Plant).
The Profile Generator transaction used to build roles, propose authorization objects via SU24, and generate the technical profiles loaded into user buffers.
Maintains check indicators and default authorization values that control what PFCG proposes when a transaction is added to a role - it does not itself enforce any runtime check.
The upgrade reconciliation transaction that merges SAP's newly shipped SU24 defaults with customer-modified entries after a support pack or release upgrade.
A child role inheriting menu and non-organizational authorizations from a master role, requiring only organizational-level field values to be entered per instance.
A container bundling single or derived roles purely for user assignment convenience - it holds no authorization data of its own.
Displays only the single most recent failed authorization check for a user - the fastest first-line diagnostic, but blind to earlier checks in the same transaction.
A scalable, authorization-specific trace capturing every check (pass or fail) over a scoped window, the modern replacement for authorization-focused ST01 tracing.
The classic system trace tool capturing authorization checks alongside DB, RFC, and other trace categories in a single unified timeline.
The User Information System - a reporting layer over current role, profile, and authorization assignment data, not a runtime trace tool.
Displays and allows reset of a user's authorization buffer without requiring a full re-login in supported scenarios.
The in-memory, session-held set of authorizations built at logon from a user's role/profile assignments, evaluated by every subsequent AUTHORITY-CHECK.
A user configured to lend its authorizations as a template to other users without those users holding the roles directly - a common audit blind spot.
A user type intended for RFC/background processing that should never be permitted interactive dialog logon.
The standard interactive user type supporting SAP GUI/Fiori logon with full password policy enforcement.
Core Data Services view - a semantically rich database view definition in S/4HANA that can carry its own row-level security via DCL annotations.
Data Control Language - defines row-level restriction rules on a CDS view, enforced independently of and in addition to classic AUTHORITY-CHECK.
The authorization mechanism for embedded/BW-style analytics restricting which aggregated data a user can see, object S_RS_AUTH.
A Fiori grouping of related apps/tiles, assigned via PFCG role menu exactly like a transaction code entry.
The Fiori-era evolution of the composite/single role pattern, bundling business catalogs and business groups for end-user assignment.
The modern Fiori Launchpad organizational structure replacing classic groups, curating tiles into role-driven personalized layouts.

An anti-cross-site-request-forgery token required for any state-changing OData call, fetched via a GET request before a POST/PUT/DELETE - a web-security control often mistaken for an authorization failure.
The REST-based protocol Fiori apps use to call backend data, exposed via SAP Gateway and secured by both SICF activation and classic authorization objects.
The transaction managing HTTP service node activation - a service must be active here before it's reachable at all, independent of authorization.
An OData mechanism bundling multiple operations into one HTTP call; each sub-operation is still independently authorization-checked, producing potentially confusing partial-failure responses.
Internet Web Framework (front-end) - the Gateway component receiving and routing incoming OData HTTP requests.
Business Enablement Provider (back-end) - the Gateway component executing the actual backend logic and authorization checks for an OData call.
The eXtension User Account and Authentication service - BTP's OAuth authorization server validating JWTs and enforcing scopes.
The finest-grained BTP permission unit, defined by a developer in xs-security.json and bundled into role templates.
An application-shipped grouping of scopes that administrators instantiate into actual Roles for their subaccount.
The BTP bundle of one or more roles, possibly spanning multiple applications, that is actually assigned to end users.
BTP's tenant-isolation boundary - each has its own XSUAA instance, trust configuration, and role collections that don't automatically propagate to other subaccounts.
JSON Web Token - the signed, scope-bearing token XSUAA issues; valid for its full lifetime regardless of subsequent role collection changes.
Identity Authentication Service - SAP's cloud identity provider, either holding users directly or brokering authentication to a corporate IdP.
IAS's risk-based rule engine requiring step-up authentication (e.g., MFA) based on signals like network location or application sensitivity - a Zero Trust building block.
An application configured to trust a given identity provider for authentication, defined via SAML2/OIDC trust configuration.
Identity Provisioning Service - automates user/group synchronization from a source system to one or more target systems.
System for Cross-domain Identity Management - the standard protocol IPS uses to create/update/deactivate users and groups on most cloud targets.
A provisioning filter design principle where ambiguous or error conditions default to excluding a user, rather than including them by default (fail-open), to avoid unintended over-provisioning.
An XML assertion-based federation protocol used for browser-based web SSO, authenticating a user and carrying attributes an application may use for authorization decisions.
The Trust Manager transaction maintaining an ABAP system's Personal Security Environment (PSE) and imported trusted certificates.
A ticket-based authentication protocol common for domain-joined desktop SAP GUI SSO, relying on SPNEGO and a correctly registered Service Principal Name.
Service Principal Name - the identifier a domain's Key Distribution Center uses to issue Kerberos service tickets for a given SAP system.
A digital certificate enabling mutual authentication; validation depends on the full certificate chain, not just the leaf certificate, being trusted.
The full sequence from leaf certificate up through intermediate CAs to a root CA - an expired intermediate breaks trust even if the leaf is still valid.
An authorization delegation protocol issuing scoped access tokens for API calls, distinct from authentication (which OpenID Connect layers on top of it).
The OAuth grant type used for Principal Propagation, where a cloud app presents a SAML assertion to obtain a backend token scoped to the original user.
The mechanism preserving a cloud-authenticated user's identity when a call reaches an on-premise backend, rather than collapsing to a generic technical user.
The on-premise agent tunneling outbound-initiated connections to a BTP subaccount, enforcing system- and resource-level access control lists.
An administrative-level HANA privilege (e.g., backup, user management) independent of and beneath any ABAP application layer.
A HANA privilege granting SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/EXECUTE on a specific schema, table, view, or procedure.
A HANA privilege restricting rows returned from a calculation/analytic view based on attribute values - enforced even for direct SQL access bypassing ABAP entirely.
HANA's column-level control returning masked/redacted values for unauthorized users without altering underlying data.
A HANA design-time, transportable role definition - the recommended approach over catalog roles for managed landscapes.
A HANA role created directly in the database, harder to transport and version-control than a repository role.
The secinfo/reginfo files managed via SMGW controlling which hosts/programs may register or call RFC function modules at the gateway level, evaluated before any application authorization.
A network proxy controlling routing between SAP systems and external networks for classic RFC/DIAG protocol traffic.
SAP's HTTP(S) load balancer and filter sitting in front of ABAP application servers - protects a different protocol than SAProuter and doesn't substitute for it.
An RFC destination configuration allowing a calling system to pass through the current user's identity without re-authenticating, restricted via S_RFCACL.
Transport Management System - governs how code and configuration changes, including authorization-relevant objects like roles and SU24 entries, move between systems.
The risk that a single user can complete both halves of a sensitive business process without independent oversight, e.g. creating a vendor and posting payment to it.
A formally documented, periodically re-certified compensating control for an SoD risk that cannot be remediated by removing access.
SAP GRC's Emergency Access Management capability granting temporary elevated access under full logging, requiring formal review within an SLA.
Logs who viewed specific sensitive fields on business objects, addressing the blind spot that display-only access can itself be a risk.
The preventive GRC workflow running risk analysis before granting new access, as opposed to only detecting violations after the fact.
A periodic detective campaign re-certifying existing user access and catching risks accumulated since the last review.

A disciplined methodology - reproduce/classify, identify recent change, trace the mechanism, apply the narrowest fix, document - for resolving production incidents correctly rather than by broad access widening.
A high-pressure incident response setting where disciplined trace-first triage is essential to avoid rushed, overly broad fixes that create larger downstream problems.
Sarbanes-Oxley - a compliance framework requiring IT general controls over financially-relevant transactions, including SoD and access review evidence.
An information security management standard whose Annex A.9 covers access control policy and least-privilege expectations relevant to SAP authorization design.
A US federal security controls catalog whose Access Control (AC) family maps closely to SAP role-based access design expectations.
A prioritized security best-practice framework whose Account and Access Control Management controls apply directly to SAP user/role governance.
The table tracking role-to-user assignments including validity dates, the authoritative source behind SUIM's assignment reports.
The table storing the actual field-level authorization values maintained within each role's authorization objects.
The customer-side tables backing SU24, storing check indicators and default authorization values per transaction-object pair.
The authorization object implicitly checked by the kernel for every transaction start, controlling which transaction codes a user may launch.
The SU24 setting (Check/Maintain, Check, No Check, Unmaintained) determining whether and how an authorization object is proposed and enforced for a transaction.
An authorization field (like company code or plant) designated for per-derived-role variation rather than being fixed in the master role.
NetWeaver Administrator - a Java-stack administration console used for monitoring and configuration in dual-stack or Java-based SAP components.
The web-based administration console for managing HANA database privileges, roles, and audit policies.
The web-based administration console for managing BTP subaccounts, role collections, and trust configuration.
The transaction maintaining RFC destinations, including stored credentials and trusted/trusting system relationships.
The Gateway monitor transaction used to review and manage secinfo/reginfo access control list configuration.
Secure Network Communications - provides encryption and stronger authentication for classic SAP GUI/RFC protocol traffic, distinct from gateway ACL enforcement.
A security model assuming no implicit trust based on network location alone, implemented in SAP landscapes via risk-based conditional authentication and least-privilege authorization design.
